food_subgroup: Fried noodle and spring roll dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers spring roll as a food product category; no specific code exists for the combined fried spring roll with vermicelli noodle dish. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers spring rolls as a category. No specific code exists for the combined bun cha gio dish with vermicelli. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Vietnamese fried spring roll with vermicelli dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Bun Cha Gio is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of rice vermicelli topped with two to three fried spring rolls, fresh herbs, and nuoc cham dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Cha Gio (Vietnamese Fried Spring Roll) and Rice Vermicelli — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Spring Rolls, Fried (NDB 35188) and Rice Noodles, Cooked (NDB 20113), weighted by typical dish composition (40% spring roll, 60% cooked vermicelli by weight)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry found for the combined Bun Cha Gio dish. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy spring roll (fried) and cooked rice vermicelli entries, weighted at approximately 40% spring roll and 60% cooked vermicelli by mass, reflecting typical restaurant plating. Nuoc cham sauce contribution to sodium is included in the estimate. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, white, boiled — GI 53 (glucose reference); analogous estimate applied to Bun Cha Gio dish given rice vermicelli as dominant carbohydrate source
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for Bun Cha Gio as a composite dish. GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled white rice noodles, which is the dominant carbohydrate component (cooked rice vermicelli, ~60% of dish by weight). The fried spring roll component contributes fat which may further moderate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as (53 × 21.5 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350g serving / 100) / 100 ≈ 20.0 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct GI testing of the composite dish has not been identified.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, seasoning, and nuoc cham dipping sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid the nuoc cham dipping sauce and request reduced fish sauce seasoning, as a standard 350 g serving may contribute approximately 1,470 mg sodium — nearly 64% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli and fried spring roll wrappers
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size, as the rice vermicelli base contributes a moderate glycemic load (GL ≈ 20 per serving); pairing with fresh herbs and limiting the sweet nuoc cham sauce can help moderate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of high sodium (~1,470 mg per serving) and saturated fat from deep-frying (approximately 6.3 g per serving) warrants portion control and preference for dishes with less frying oil and reduced dipping sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Cha Gio (Fried Spring Roll with Vermicelli) is a popular Vietnamese dish consisting of a bowl of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bun) served alongside or topped with crispy deep-fried spring rolls (cha gio). The spring rolls are typically filled with a mixture of seasoned pork mince, glass noodles, wood ear mushrooms, and finely shredded carrots, wrapped in rice paper and deep-fried until golden and crunchy. The dish is served with fresh herbs such as mint and lettuce, bean sprouts, and a nuoc cham dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. In Singapore, this dish is offered at Vietnamese restaurants such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant and is classified as a snack or light meal. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~175 kcal per 100 g), a reasonable protein contribution from the pork filling (~6.5 g per 100 g), and carbohydrates primarily from the rice vermicelli (~21.5 g per 100 g). The deep-frying process adds fat (~7.2 g per 100 g) and the fish sauce and nuoc cham contribute significantly to sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low-medium range) based on the rice noodle component. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its glycemic load, and of high concern for hypertension due to sodium from fish sauce and dipping sauce. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to pork and fish sauce content.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry was found for the composite Bun Cha Gio dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for fried spring rolls and cooked rice vermicelli, weighted by typical dish composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice noodles. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: fried spring rolls (~40% by weight) and cooked rice vermicelli (~60% by weight). No single approved-source entry for the complete Bun Cha Gio dish was identified. 2. Sodium estimate includes a contribution from nuoc cham dipping sauce; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on sauce quantity consumed. 3. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice noodles; no direct GI measurement for the composite dish was found. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite estimate. 6. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains pork; it may be available in non-pork variants at some establishments. 7. The cuisine is classified as Vietnamese as the dish originates from Vietnamese culinary tradition, though it is served in Singapore. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as the dish language of origin. 8. aliases_by_language Hindi entry uses Devanagari script: 'तले हुए स्प्रिंग रोल और वर्मीसेली' and 'वर्मीसेली के साथ तले हुए स्प्रिंग रोल' — both strings verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English food name is precise. Vietnamese local name (Bún Chả Giò) is present. Singaporean dish multilingual requirements (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are satisfied. Vietnamese origin name also provided.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros (energy_kcal 175.0, protein_g 6.5, fat_g 7.2, carbohydrate_g 21.5) are present, numeric, and plausible. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with explicit composite estimation methodology documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.9), sodium_mg (420.0), saturated_fat_g (1.8), and sugar_g (1.8) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for this dish type.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (53), gi_category (low), glycemic_load_per_serving (20.0), and gi_source are all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database as analogous estimate for boiled white rice noodles, documented clearly.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 (spring roll, closest_parent) and FoodEx2 A0FKP (Spring rolls, closest_parent) are defensible anchors. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters). It covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, glycemic load), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, sodium and saturated fat). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Comprehensive audit performed: every string in aliases_by_language verified to exist verbatim in the flat aliases list. Tamil entries ('வறுத்த ஸ்பிரிங் ரோல் மற்றும் வெர்மிசெல்லி', 'தலை வறுத்த ஸ்பிரிங் ரோல்'), Hindi entries ('तले हुए स्प्रिंग रोல और वर्मीसेली', 'वर्मीसेली के साथ तले हुए स्प்रிंग रोल'), Chinese entries, Malay entry, Vietnamese entries, and romanized entries all confirmed present verbatim in flat aliases. The Hindi entry in aliases_by_language uses the exact same mixed Devanagari string as in the flat aliases list.
